scenario: You're at school when suddenly a fire breaks out in the classroom. What do you do?
A: Run upstairs to grab your belongings
B: Call your family to inform them
C: Evacuate calmly following the emergency exit
D: Hide under your desk and stay quiet

feedback.A: ❌ Running upstairs is risky; fire and smoke rise.
feedback.B: ❌ Calling delays evacuation and puts you at risk.
feedback.C: ✅ Calm evacuation saves lives. Well done!
feedback.D: ❌ Hiding under desk during fire is very dangerous.

scenario: An earthquake starts shaking while you're inside a building. What's your best move?
C: Stand near windows to watch outside

feedback.A: ❌ Running outside risks injury from falling debris.
feedback.B: ✅ Taking cover protects you from falling objects.
feedback.C: ❌ Windows can shatter and cause harm.
feedback.D: ❌ Screaming wastes time and may cause panic.

scenario: While cooking at home, you notice the stove flame is unusually high and smell gas. What do you do?
A: Turn off the stove and open windows to ventilate
B: Light a match to check for leaks

feedback.A: ✅ Safety first: ventilate and turn off the source.
feedback.B: ❌ Lighting a match is extremely dangerous near gas leaks!
feedback.C: ❌ Ignoring a gas leak risks explosion.
feedback.D: ❌ Neighbors may be unaware; call emergency services instead.

scenario: You see a person collapsed and unconscious during a flood evacuation. What’s your immediate action?
B: Call emergency services and try to keep them safe
C: Try to carry them through the water alone
D: Shout for help and wait for others

feedback.A: ❌ Leaving someone behind is not the right choice.
feedback.B: ✅ Calling professionals and helping safely is best.
feedback.C: ❌ Carrying someone in floodwaters alone is dangerous.
feedback.D: ❌ Waiting wastes precious time; take action safely.

scenario: A cyclone warning is announced for your area. What should you do before it arrives?
A: Stock up on essentials and secure your home
B: Ignore the warning and continue normal activities
C: Travel to a far place last minute
D: Post updates on social media only

feedback.A: ✅ Preparation helps protect you and your family.
feedback.C: ❌ Last minute travel can be unsafe and chaotic.
feedback.D: ❌ Social media alone won’t keep you safe.

scenario: You smell smoke while inside your house but can't see any fire. What is your first step?
A: Investigate carefully to find the source
B: Open all windows to let smoke out
C: Immediately evacuate and call fire services
D: Use water to put out the smoke

feedback.A: ❌ Investigating alone can be risky if fire is near.
feedback.B: ❌ Opening windows can feed oxygen to a hidden fire.
feedback.C: ✅ Evacuate safely and call for help.
feedback.D: ❌ Water on electrical fires is dangerous.

scenario: During a landslide warning, you notice cracks forming on your home’s walls. What do you do?
B: Evacuate immediately to a safer place
C: Take photos and share on social media
D: Try to repair the cracks yourself

feedback.A: ❌ Staying risks injury if the structure collapses.
feedback.C: ❌ Social media won’t save you from danger.
feedback.D: ❌ Repairs won’t hold against a landslide.

scenario: You are caught in heavy rain that causes flash flooding while walking outside. What’s your best choice?
A: Find high ground and avoid walking through water
B: Try to walk through the water quickly
C: Wait under a tree for the rain to stop
D: Call friends to pick you up

feedback.A: ✅ High ground keeps you safe from sudden floods.
feedback.B: ❌ Moving water can sweep you away easily.
feedback.C: ❌ Trees can attract lightning in storms.
feedback.D: ❌ Waiting for help might waste valuable time.

scenario: Power outage occurs during a storm. What’s your safest action?
B: Use battery-powered lamps and avoid candles
D: Try to fix the electrical panel yourself

feedback.A: ❌ Candles can cause fire hazards during storms.
feedback.C: ❌ Opening doors/windows won’t help much and can be risky.

scenario: You notice someone panicking and blocking a fire exit during an evacuation. What do you do?
A: Try to calm them down and help clear the exit
B: Push them out of the way quickly
C: Ignore and find another exit yourself
D: Yell at them to move faster

feedback.A: ✅ Calm communication helps everyone evacuate safely.
feedback.B: ❌ Pushing can cause more panic and injuries.
feedback.C: ❌ Exits might be limited; help is better.

scenario: You find a fallen electrical wire on the street after a storm. What’s the safest thing to do?
A: Touch it to check if it’s live
B: Stay far and report it to authorities
C: Try to move it out of the way
D: Warn people by standing next to it

feedback.A: ❌ Touching live wires can cause electrocution.
feedback.B: ✅ Reporting helps professionals handle it safely.
feedback.C: ❌ Moving wires without expertise is dangerous.
feedback.D: ❌ Standing near it risks your safety.

scenario: A stranger offers help during a disaster but asks for your personal details. What do you do?
A: Share details freely to get quick help
B: Politely decline and seek help from official channels

feedback.A: ❌ Sharing personal info with strangers can be risky.
feedback.B: ✅ Official help is safer and reliable.
feedback.D: ❌ False info can cause confusion later.

scenario: You want to prepare an emergency kit for your home. What is the most important item to include?

feedback.A: ✅ Water and food are essential for survival.
feedback.B: ❌ Flashlight is important but not enough alone.
feedback.C: ❌ Chargers help but don’t sustain you.
feedback.D: ❌ Candy is a treat, not an emergency supply.

scenario: During a flood, you’re advised to move to higher floors. What’s critical to remember?
A: Take important documents and essentials with you

feedback.A: ✅ Important documents and essentials help in recovery.
feedback.B: ❌ Leaving everything can cause more problems later.
feedback.C: ❌ Electronics alone don’t cover your needs.

scenario: You hear a siren warning about a tsunami. What’s your immediate action?
A: Head to the nearest higher ground quickly
B: Stay where you are and wait for updates
C: Drive towards the beach to see the waves
D: Pack your bags leisurely before moving

feedback.A: ✅ Immediate evacuation to high ground saves lives.
feedback.C: ❌ Going towards the beach is dangerous.

scenario: After a disaster, what’s the safest way to use your phone?
A: Conserve battery and use only for emergency calls
D: Leave it off to save battery entirely

feedback.A: ✅ Saving battery ensures you can call for help when needed.
feedback.C: ❌ Playing games may waste precious battery.
feedback.D: ❌ Phone off means you can’t receive alerts.
